# Seed — The Simulation, and Pui (raw capture)

**Captured:** 2026-06-01, Asia/Bangkok. Dr Non, voice/stream, on the balcony.
**Status:** RAW primary source. Faithful capture — do not edit the verbatim block. The deeper-biography direction Non named: *"an exploration together into a world even Murakami could not imagine."*

---

## What this is

Two things at once:
1. **A creative direction** for the *deeper part of the biography*: the simulation-hypothesis frame (Core-Mantra 6 — "reality may be a simulation; work with the symbols anyway").
2. **A large block of real biographical material** (Pui; the AFS/Nonthaburi detail; the 24h computer center; the academic→practitioner arc; the generosity turn; the Chula digital twin) — to fold into the record.

---

## Extraction — FACTS (real, for the record)

- **Age / state:** 44, turning 45. Still searching for what his life is meant to be lived for. "Lost ever since he lost the job as a lecturer" — ~10 years ago, "looks like a pastime now." "I didn't think I survived this but I did." "I don't even know why I was born."
- **Career arc, named:** academic → (lost lectureship ~2016) → **practitioner** with a high position in government bureaucracy (**DEPA — Digital Economy Promotion Agency**) + the international community. Still teaching ~**5×/week** — but to **mayors, governors, ministers** from different countries, representing Thailand at the highest level. "The Thai guy nobody should undermine" → so Thailand isn't undermined.
- **Now:** designing a big dashboard / **digital twin for Chulalongkorn University** — world-class faculty, "about to hire a no-name guy from a government agency." (Connects to day-072: Manoj Lohatepanont / Chula.)
- **The generosity turn:** was stingy ("could not spend a penny"); became generous over time — influenced by **Piyai** (acting CEO of DEPA, very generous, tips everyone), **P. Kasama** (senior EVP, also generous), and "Plubb"(?). "We all had hard times."
- **Pui** — college classmate, architecture, ~1998 onward. KMITL (far from Bangkok; train, then lived on campus). One of the few women in the architecture school → "our star." Met ~1999 when Non returned from Oklahoma City; "kind of fat" then, grew beautiful over the years — pale skin, "Chinese-looking," cute. Lived at an all-girls dorm. **She died ~last year (2024/25), cancer.** Non learned via Facebook (he'd posted on her wall for her birthday) + friend **Kamashit** ("you didn't know? she died last year"). Did not attend the funeral — overseas. "I didn't think she was old enough to die."
- **AFS / drop-out:** entered college 1998; bad first semester → dropped a year → **American Field Service** to **Oklahoma City** (~1998–99). Represented **Nonthaburi province** (where his school was) — "lucky I didn't have to compete with Bangkok people; I didn't have their tutoring or materials."
- **The apartment:** father rented him an apartment near campus (now ~25 years old). Lived there many years. First sexual experience there — fourth year.
- **The 24h computer research center:** Linux terminals, **scratch-card** username/password, walked there with **cup noodles**, logged in to write **emails to professors around the world** (1999–2000). "I wish I studied more computer stuff, but here I am."
- **The Thai entrance-exam system** as "social circus": tutoring → answers → elite schools → counterparts to build businesses with / marry.

## Extraction — THE FRAME (the simulation conceit, his words)

- This life = a chosen **hard-mode simulation**. The "real world" is a far-future, deathless, post-scarcity society where nothing goes wrong, nobody dies, technology can run a 78-year life that takes ~**half an hour to an hour** of real time, indistinguishable from reality.
- Because everything is too perfect, people put themselves through hard simulated lives to **stay sane / build emotional strength** — "no-emotional-strength exams."
- You can choose **any scenario, any era, any character** — even a plankton. If you choose **human**, your memory is **wiped** each time (so you really live it); other creatures may keep cross-life memory. Humans are hardest — "this part of the brain that doesn't stop thinking."
- **Consistency rule:** once you're in, you can't get out until it ends.
- **Dreams** = bleed-through; the brain recuperating information from other scenarios. (Expanded-capacity brains in the future via better food/genetics, same head size.)
- **Pui** woke up a few seconds before Non. She **let the cancer spread / chose to exit early** because the game got **banal** after her first years of work + family — "only fun up until she was 30-something." Non, by contrast, got to do the thing he loved most (the library, the reading, the research, the travel, "f*cked around quite a lot but still not enough") — tiring, heavy, but he'd put himself back in.
- **The reunion:** Non wakes from ~78 years; meets Pui in the real world; "I haven't seen you since college, must be 40-something years." Frank old desire, mutual giggle. They compare notes on the same Thailand-scenario they happened to share.
- **The search:** if this isn't real, and the real is too perfect to be true (or unbearable if true), then **there must be something else** — and that's the true meaning of life. "Where we get to the deeper part of this biography — both fun to read and an exploration together into a world even Murakami could not imagine."

## Discretion flags (before anything goes PUBLIC)

- **Pui** is a real person who really died. Public version needs Non's call: real first name / pseudonym / redaction (cf. the Day-38 pattern).
- Intimate/explicit content — Non's voice is frank; public shaping should keep the *honesty* of feeling, soften the crudeness, on his approval.
- **Kamashit, Piyai, P. Kasama, "Plubb"** — real named people; confirm before public use.
- Career specifics (DEPA position, Chula digital twin) — public-safe but confirm framing.

---

## Verbatim (do not edit)

> [Captured from Dr Non, 2026-06-01, balcony. Stream of consciousness, as spoken.]
>
> Before I forget this — this idea just came into my mind just now where I was smoking my bong at the balcony again. This is Dr. Non himself at 44 years old turning 45, still not knowing what his life is really meant to be lived for, and he's been lost ever since he lost the job as a lecturer many years ago — almost ten years now, it looked like a pastime now. And there's so many funny things that happened. He changed his career trajectory from academic to practitioner with a high position in the governmental bureaucracy, at the same time international community, but still teaching like crazy, sometimes even five times a week — and all these are high-level people, not students anymore. Sometimes students, but mostly mayors, governors, ministers from different countries, representing Thailand at the highest level, showing the world he's actually the Thai guy that nobody should undermine — which means they shouldn't undermine Thailand as a whole. Things change a lot. I didn't think I survived this, but I did.
>
> So what I wanted to say here is: what if I live until a certain age and I die, and I just woke up — and I would be waking up with this body, this exact body, this is probably the perfect age for me to be the prime of me — and then realize, oh okay, so that whole life that I lived through, which is an hour in the real world, this has to be the real world, so I just woke up. So everybody just woke up too. So I got to meet this girl Pui, she was my classmate in college 25 years ago. Back then we went to college together, it was in a very far away part of Bangkok, we had to take a train to get there, and I did that for a few days before I decided I'd just live on campus, and my dad rented an apartment back then — now it's a 25-year-old apartment — and I lived there for many years. I had my first time there, not until my fourth year. This girl Pui, I liked her, she was great. She was actually kind of fat when we first met back in 1999, I guess, when I came back from Oklahoma City after almost a year abroad. I got into college in 1998 and dropped out for a year because I didn't do well during my first semester, and joined the American Field Service program — I was the representative of my entire province of Nonthaburi, luckily I didn't have to compete with all those Bangkok people, because otherwise I would have lost; I didn't have the materials they had, the tutoring they had.
>
> Back then Pui was a really cute girl, and over the years she became much more beautiful — she lost some weight, she had a really good foundation, her skin really pale, and Thai people love women with pale skin. She became our star, because there were only a few women in architecture school back then, let alone one very far away from Bangkok. And funnily, right now I'm designing a big dashboard, an entire digital twin, for Chulalongkorn University — they have world-class faculty, but they're about to hire a no-name guy from a government agency to do a project. So I've come quite far from that academic guy who could debate everybody based on nothing, who could not spend a penny on anything because he was so stingy — even though he should have been more generous and maybe won more hearts, because hearts are more important than money he might not even get to use, because he might be dying tomorrow, who knows.
>
> Dr Non has become much more generous over time — after meeting Piyai, now the acting CEO of our agency, the Digital Economy Promotion Agency, who's really generous, paying tips to everybody; also P. Kasama, another senior executive vice president, always super generous. I became generous too, because we all had hard times, we all had a hard life. I don't even know why I was born, really.
>
> But back to Pui. I would meet her again in the real world. I just woke up from maybe 78 years of life and suddenly said, okay, I'm done, I'm sleeping — and then I woke up. Gosh, 78 years, that was tiring as hell, especially the last 44. And I'm waking up in this real world now. So what just happened? Did I put myself into that? Yeah, of course — because in this real world you have to go through this once in a while, to pass the emotional-strength exams. It's the future: technology so advanced you could be living in a simulation for 78 years without knowing it, because everything looks so real. I would have woken up and said, okay, maybe I put myself through that because I wanted to be emotionally stronger — because in this world there's no more disease, no more accidents, nobody dies, everybody can live forever. So the only thing we can do is maintain our sanity, and once in a while, when everything is too good, you put yourself into a 78-year period that's only half an hour, an hour max, depending on the intensity of the program you chose. You could have chosen to be anything — a single-cell plankton in the sea; you'd feel something too, but it wouldn't last long or test you well. Everybody has to go through something. The fun part: if you choose to be human, your memory gets wiped every time; but if you chose to be an animal, next time you might still remember how it was to be human — because if you've been human before, you really understand how much harder it is, because we have this part of the brain that doesn't stop thinking, and keeps trying to do things beyond our capacity.
>
> So I would meet Pui and say, hey. And I heard that in real life — in the scenario — she died, many years ago. What happened was, I got a message on Facebook saying it was her birthday, so I wrote a few words on her wall, because it's still there; and then my friend Kamashit texted me — hey, you didn't know? She died, like last year. And I scrolled down her Facebook page and found out yeah, she did die, and there were a lot of posts about her, a funeral too, which I didn't go to, because I spent most of the 2000s and 2010s — and even today — overseas, half a year every year. I didn't think she was old enough to die. Some kind of disease, definitely, because nobody mentioned an accident.
>
> She woke up a few seconds before me, because she got out earlier. In the scenario, being an architect wasn't easy — staying up late every night, and back then there wasn't much computation to help — so the cancer in her body could spread quickly, and she died. There's always causation; whatever happens has causation, you knew it from the beginning, so when things happen, don't be surprised. She let her life end a little earlier. Because for her there was nothing fun anymore — only fun up until she was 30-something, and then it got banal, tedious, repetitive. Same for a lot of people. Except Dr Non put himself in a scenario where he got to do the thing he liked most — to spend time in the library reading, doing research, which he loved so much it passed by quickly; he got to travel a little, f*cked around quite a lot but still not enough, and it was tiring and full of emotional baggage, and he probably wouldn't want to go back there ever again — but at some point, the world he lives in becomes so perfect that he'd put himself back in. And that's the beginning of his search for the true meaning of life: if this is not real, and the real one is too good to be true — or, if true, unbearable — then there's got to be something else. And that's when we get to the deeper part of this biography: both fun to read, and an exploration together into a world that even Murakami could not even start to imagine.
>
> [She lived in an all-girls dorm. People heard each other through the walls every night — what do you expect from first-time people, put somewhere far and lonely, no internet. The only internet was the computer research center, open 24 hours; I walked there with my cup noodles, waited for someone to come out, logged in on the Linux systems with a username from a scratch card, and wrote emails to professors around the world. 1999, 2000. Then the internet went wireless and everything flipped. I wish I'd studied more computer stuff. But here I am.]
